From Targeted to Universal Agents

Outline (From Targeted to Universal Agents)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [5]
  3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [6]
  4. Agent Skills [5]
  5. Declarative Workflows [7]
  6. Wrap up [2]
From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(6) The Age of Universal Agents

  • Agents are evolving from single-purpose tools to broad-capability systems
  • Each new capability adds tools and decisions to the agent's scope
    • More capabilities →
    • More decisions →
    • More room for error
  • The question is not whether agents can do more, it's how to make them do it
    • reliably
    • repeatably
    • efficiently


From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(7) The Tool Problem

  • Tool overload: too many options degrade agent decision quality
  • Tool selection is itself a reasoning task and it fails at scale
  • Three patterns address this directly: MCP, Agent Skills, and (Arazzo) Workflows
  • We will use a single scenario throughout: planning a spontaneous weekend getaway — weather check, hotel search, booking, and calendar — across three levels of agent tooling


From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(8) The Reproducibility Ladder

Four approaches to agent capability with increasing determinism at each rung:

Prompting MCP Agent Skills Workflows (Arazzo)
Execution LLM Tool engine LLM (guided) Workflow engine
LLM role Full Discovery & Invocation Within constraints Discovery & Invocation*
Reproducibility Low Medium Medium High

* LLMs can also author and register (Arazzo) workflows by converting observed processes into reusable, deterministic assets stored for future agent use.



From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(9) Agent Maintainability

  • Agents are software that needs maintenance over time
  • Agents run on LLMs which make them both powerful and shape-shifting
    • The same inputs produce different outputs when the model changes
    • Keeping the model stable may be difficult or impossible
  • Evals are good and useful but hard to do for universal agents


From Targeted to Universal Agents Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(10) Demo: Weekend Getaway Planner



Model Context Protocol (MCP)

Outline (Model Context Protocol (MCP))

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [5]
  3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [6]
  4. Agent Skills [5]
  5. Declarative Workflows [7]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(12) Model Context Protocol (MCP)

  • MCP is the current standard protocol for LLM consumers
  • The initial focus was on additional and extended context
    • Additional context by providing access to the environment
    • Extended context by allowing MCP servers to externally manage context
  • Many mapping tools generate MCP servers from OpenAPI descriptions
  • What to expose and how to design it is out of scope


Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(13) MCP all the APIs

  1. Generate MCP from OpenAPI
  2. Publish/Install MCP servers for all APIs
  3. Consumers can access all MCP tools


Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(14) MCP all the APIs: Easy but Limited

  • Pros:
    • Zero development effort, many tools auto-generate MCP
    • All APIs easily available
    • Great for quick and focused experimentation
  • Cons:
    • Context window overflow happens quickly
    • AI struggles with understanding workflows across endpoints
    • Risk of misinterpreting MCP problems as general AI problems
  • Good as a starting point but lacks sophistication and scalability


Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(15) The API Landscape Complexity Challenge

  • GitHub API [https://github.com/github/rest-api-description]
    • 11MB
    • 320k lines
    • 738 paths
    • 1114 operations
    • 259 webhooks
  • GitHub MCP Server [https://github.com/github/github-mcp-server]
    • 19 toolsets (+3 remote)
    • 70 tools overall


Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(16) Demo 1: MCP Tools — Capable but Non-deterministic

  • 7 hand-crafted tools across 4 APIs: weather · hotel search · booking · calendar
  • The LLM decides: which tool to call first · how to pass data between calls · when conditions are met


Model Context Protocol (MCP) Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(17) Demo 1



Agent Skills

Outline (Agent Skills)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [5]
  3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [6]
  4. Agent Skills [5]
  5. Declarative Workflows [7]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(19) What Is an Agent Skill?

  • Structured, reusable context for a specific subtask
  • Lives in the LLM's context window — shapes how it thinks and acts
  • Explicit scope: does one thing, with guardrails, in a defined way
  • Versioned artifact — reviewed, shared, and reused across agents

At this rung: We load the weekend-getaway-planner skill. The LLM knows exactly which tools to call, in what order, with what thresholds. Consistent output. Reusable across agents.



Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(20) Anatomy of a Skill

A SKILL.md defines: task · constraints · steps · references to external scripts or tools

  • Task: what the skill is for — clear, bounded scope
  • Constraints: what the LLM must and must not do
  • Steps: how to approach the task, in order
  • References: external scripts, tools, or MCP capabilities to invoke

Versioned, shareable, and loadable by any compatible agent. Think of it as a reusable prompt with structure and guardrails baked in.

A skill tells the LLM how to think about a task. A skill can discover and run a workflow — once it does, the LLM is out of the execution loop.



Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(21) Agent Skills: When to Use

  • Use when: recurring subtask needing LLM judgment · consistent output required · reusability matters
  • Not for: multi-step processes with a defined sequence · capabilities needed by multiple systems
  • Reproducibility: Medium — guided, not guaranteed


Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(22) Demo 2: Agent Skill — Guided Execution

  • Same 7 MCP tools. One addition: SKILL.md loaded into the agent's context
  • Maintainability win: update one skill file, not every prompt that references it


Agent Skills Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(23) Demo 2



Declarative Workflows

Outline (Declarative Workflows)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [5]
  3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [6]
  4. Agent Skills [5]
  5. Declarative Workflows [7]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(25) The Problem Workflows Solve

Problem 1 — Non-determinism:

  • Same prompt, three runs, three outcomes. Token waste. No audit trail.
  • At one agent, a bug. At a hundred, a governance crisis.

Problem 2 — MCP catalog sprawl:

  • As repeatable processes multiply, so does the server catalog
  • You have traded LLM unpredictability for infrastructure maintenance

Can you describe the process once, run it identically every time, and not maintain a server to do it?



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(26) What Is Arazzo?

  • Declarative: define the sequence, data flow, and success criteria
  • OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) [https://www.openapis.org/] standard for multi-step API workflows
  • Arazzo workflows reference operationIds from OpenAPI/AsyncAPI descriptions
  • Discoverable by agents (via MCP or as APIs): find and run a workflow without knowing its internals 
workflows:
  - workflowId: weekend-getaway
    steps:
      - stepId: listHotels
        description: Retrieve hotels available in the destination city
        operationPath: '{$sourceDescriptions.liteapi-hotel-data.url}#/paths/~1data~1hotels/get'
        parameters:
          - name: cityName
            in: query
            value: $inputs.cityName
        successCriteria:
          - condition: $statusCode == 200
        outputs:
          hotelIds: $response.body#/data[*]/hotelId
  • arazzo.yaml

See the full workflow: arazzo-ui.jentic.com [https://arazzo-ui.jentic.com/?document=https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Ffrankkilcommins%2Fagentic-tools-webinar%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Farazzo%2Fweekend-getaway.arazzo.yaml]



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(27) Workflows: When to Use

  • Use when: multi-step process · defined sequence · auditability required · enterprise or regulated context
  • Not for: exploratory tasks · tasks needing LLM judgment at each step
  • Reproducibility: High


Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(28) Demo 3: Arazzo Workflow — One Tool, Deterministic

  • The full sequence encoded in a single Arazzo document [https://arazzo-ui.jentic.com/?document=https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Ffrankkilcommins%2Fagentic-tools-webinar%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Farazzo%2Fweekend-getaway.arazzo.yaml] — exposed as one MCP tool
  • Maintainability win: the workflow is the artefact — version it, test it, share it like any other API contract


Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(29) Demo 3



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(30) Should You Build an Agent? Now Answer It.

Based on Anthropic's Should I Build an Agent? framework — extended with the full toolkit:

Question Coarse answer With the full toolkit
Is the task complex enough? No → Workflow No → Arazzo. Yes but needs context → Skill first, leveraging MCP tools as needed; once the path is known, graduate to Arazzo.
Is the task valuable enough? <$0.10/run → Workflow · >$1 → Agent Low-cost repeatable → Arazzo. Needs LLM judgment each time → Skill.
Are all steps known and fixed? Yes → Workflow Yes → Arazzo. Context-dependent → Skill guides, Arazzo executes.
What is the cost of error? High → Workflow + human gate High → Arazzo with decision points. Shared capability → MCP.

The question was never just agent vs. workflow. It was: what level of determinism does this task actually need?



Declarative Workflows Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(31) How They Layer: The Full Picture

Prompting MCP Agent Skills Workflows (Arazzo)
Execution LLM Tool engine LLM (guided) Workflow engine
LLM role Full Discovery & Invocation Within constraints Discovery & Invocation
Reproducibility Low Medium Medium High
Maintenance None Medium (servers) Low Low (declarative)
Best for Exploration Shared capabilities Guided reasoning Repeatable processes
Failure mode Non-determinism Catalog sprawl Drift Scope rigidity

Key distinction: A skill tells the LLM how to think about a task (flexible path). A workflow tells the system what to do, step by step, without asking the LLM (fixed path). They compose: skills discover and run workflows.



Wrap up

Outline (Wrap up)

  1. Introduction [2]
  2. From Targeted to Universal Agents [5]
  3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) [6]
  4. Agent Skills [5]
  5. Declarative Workflows [7]
  6. Wrap up [2]
Wrap up Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(33) What to Do Next

  1. Start with your highest-value repeatable task — run it with raw MCP tools first, add a skill, then graduate to Arazzo as the sequence stabilises
  2. Identify your capabilities — where are you on the ladder? What level of reproducibility does each task actually need?
  3. Ask: who else needs this capability? — if it's agents only, Arazzo may be enough; if humans, other systems, and agents all need it, you need more than just MCP
  4. Highest-risk recurring task → write a skill for it
  5. Capability needed by multiple systems → wrap it in MCP
  6. Multi-step business process → describe it in Arazzo


Wrap up Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents

(34) Thank You! Let's Connect!
